label: Developing the Clinical Teacher (ticket)
notes: Turas SLE TICKET for Developing the Clinical Teacher — the trainee REQUESTS the SLE; the assessor completes it later, so there are no ratings or assessor-feedback fields here. ASP.NET MVC. The ticket page hosts all four SLE types at once, each behind a SelectedForms_XxxFormTicketed checkbox with its own prefixed fields (ClinicalTeacherTitle, ClinicalTeacherHistory...), so we tick our form's box first and fill only its block. Yes/No radios share one id and differ only by value=True/False — matched by SELECTOR per option, never by id. The Recipient (assessor) list is ACCOUNT-SPECIFIC (real supervisor names + Turas ids), so it is reference-only in v1: the user picks it on the form. Dates are dd/mm/yyyy (the input carries data-val ukdateparser). REFLECTION: auto-filled from July 2026. Claude rewrites what the trainee dictated into polished, professional reflective prose — faithful tidying, NOT generation. Every fact, insight and learning point must trace back to what was actually said; nothing is invented; nothing dictated = empty box. The trainee reviews it on Turas before sending, as always.

fields.label: Ticket a Developing the Clinical Teacher
fields.extractionHints: Always Yes — this ticket is for a Developing the Clinical Teacher.

fields.label: Date of event
fields.extractionHints: The date the event took place. Accept spoken dates.

fields.label: Title
fields.extractionHints: A short title for the case, e.g. 'Chest pain in a 60-year-old'. NO patient identifiers.

fields.label: Setting
fields.extractionHints: Ward based, Journal club, Lecture, Tutorial, or Other.

fields.label: Other clinical setting
fields.extractionHints: Only if the setting is Other.

fields.label: More detail of setting
fields.extractionHints: Optional extra detail about where it happened.

fields.label: Virtual teaching event?
fields.extractionHints: Was this a virtual teaching event?

fields.label: Audience
fields.extractionHints: Who you taught (several allowed): medical students, foundation doctors, mixed medical, MDT, other.

fields.label: Case history / summary
fields.extractionHints: The account of the case — what happened, what you did. This is the main narrative the assessor will discuss with you. NO patient identifiers (no names, hospital numbers, DOB).

fields.label: Reflection
fields.extractionHints:
The trainee's reflection on this case.

You are writing up the trainee's reflection so it reads as a strong, professional piece of reflective writing — the standard expected in a UK surgical training portfolio.

WRITE IT WELL. This is the main task.
• Use the GMC's reflective arc: WHAT happened that mattered · SO WHAT it meant (why it was significant, what you recognised) · WHAT NOW (what you take forward, what you will do differently).
• Focus on the LEARNING, not the story. GMC guidance is explicit: a reflective note should set out what you learnt and what you will do, not re-narrate the case. Keep case description to the minimum needed to make the learning make sense.
• Write in connected, considered prose — first person, past tense for events, professional register. Not bullet points, not tidied speech, not a transcript with the "um"s removed.
• Develop the trainee's thinking properly: draw out the insight they expressed, make its significance explicit, and state the consequence for their practice. Give each idea the sentences it deserves.
• Be specific. "I will use ultrasound guidance at first review" is reflection; "I will be more careful" is not.
• Structure is a scaffold, not a straitjacket — if the material doesn't fit the arc neatly, write it in whatever shape serves the thinking best.

THE ONE BOUNDARY — EVERY IDEA MUST BE THEIRS.
You are expressing the trainee's thinking fully and well. You are not supplying it. This note goes into a training record under their name and is read by an ARCP panel as evidence of THEIR reflective practice.
• Every learning point, insight, judgement, feeling and clinical fact must trace back to something the trainee actually said. Elaborate their idea; never introduce a new one.
• You may develop, sharpen and articulate a thought they expressed — that is the job. You may not conclude something they did not conclude.
• Do NOT add clinical facts, outcomes or complications they did not mention.
• Do NOT attribute feelings they did not express.
• Do NOT reach for stock reflective themes (human factors, situational awareness, team dynamics, "this highlighted the importance of communication") unless that is genuinely what they told you.
• If they said nothing that answers this question, return an EMPTY string. An empty box is honest; a fabricated reflection is not.

So: take what they gave you, however scrappy, and write the best, sharpest, most professional version of THAT.

fields.label: Assessor (recipient)
fields.extractionHints: Who you're sending the ticket to. The list is specific to your Turas account, so it's shown for reference only — you pick them on the form.

fields.label: Comments to assessor
fields.extractionHints: Any message to the assessor with the ticket.
